content:
First we label the elements of M in increasing order by  
 M₁=1, M₂=2, M₃=4, M₄=5, M₅=7, M₆=8,…  

One checks that for k≥1  
 M₂k₋₁=3(k–1)+1, M₂k=3(k–1)+2.  

We seek 2n consecutive terms whose sum is 300.  There are two “alignment”‐cases depending on whether we start at an odd or an even index.  In each case one finds an equation of the form  
 n·(n+2m)=100  or  n·(n+2m+1)=100,  (m≥0 integer).  
Testing all positive divisors n of 100 and requiring m≥0 integral forces  

Case 1 (start at an odd index) ⇒ n(n+2m)=100 gives n=2 or n=10.  
Case 2 (start at an even index) ⇒ n(n+2m+1)=100 gives n=1,4,5.  

Hence the only possible n are  
 n=1, 2, 4, 5, 10.  

Answer:  \boxed{1,2,4,5,10}
